summary: Dan Luu challenges Joel Spolsky's famous claim that great developers are nearly impossible to hire because they're never on the market. Luu argues this model contradicts itself—if great developers are easy to identify, companies would simply poach them. Through empirical observations, he demonstrates that the hiring market isn't a 'market for lemons' as commonly believed, but rather that hiring difficulties stem from four fixable problems: paying too little, filtering out good candidates through arbitrary criteria, seeking rare skill combinations, or being more dysfunctional than management realizes. The real scarcity isn't great developers, but rather great teams and environments worth joining.
key_points: Joel Spolsky's model assumes companies can easily identify great developers and retain them, which creates a logical contradiction—if identification is easy, poaching should be common
Information asymmetries often favor external employers over internal management, as demonstrated by engineers doing critical work that goes unrecognized internally
Good teams are harder to find than good developers—great teams fill up quickly through networks while dysfunctional teams constantly hire
Most hiring difficulties stem from four solvable problems: low compensation, arbitrary filtering, rare skill requirements, or unrecognized dysfunction
Companies systematically filter out qualified candidates through irrelevant criteria (pedigree, traditional interview performance) while fighting over the same small pool of 'trendy' candidates
money_quotes: It's actually quite common for prospective employers to have better information than current employers!
If you join a random team, you're overwhelmingly likely to join a team that has a lot of churn. Additionally, if you know of a good team, it's likely to be full.
Companies always seem to think that they pay well, even when they're not even close to being in the right range.
I've never worked at a place that doesn't filter out a lot of people who end up doing great work elsewhere.

There's often a long time period where a 'great' dev has an extremely hard time getting hired, even through their network who knows that they're great, because they don't look like what people think 'great' developers look like.
key_insight: The hiring crisis in tech is largely self-inflicted—companies systematically underpay and filter out qualified candidates using arbitrary criteria, then complain about talent scarcity while great developers struggle to find good teams worth joining.
